F P P C

JAN 27 11 23 AM '83

City of Concord

PHONE: (415) 671 3160                                    CITY COUNCIL
                                                         Diane Longshore, Mayor
Office of City Attorney                                  Stephen L. Weir, Vice Mayor
                                                         June V. Bulman
                                                         Colleen Coll
                                                         Ronald K. Mullin
January 25, 1983                                         Farrel A. Stewart, City Manager


Janis Shank McLean, Counsel
Legal Division
State of California Fair
Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Re:  Request for Advice on Employment of Diane Longshore by
     Bay Area Council (Your Advice No. A-82-211)

Dear Ms. McLean:

     In regard to your letter of January 19, 1983, you have indicated
in the first paragraph that Mayor Longshore is employed by the
Bay Area Council.  I presume that you reached this conclusion
because of our conversations in which I indicated that if she
were to accept this employment she would begin work by January
3.  However, due to the fact that it would not be possible to get
your advice letter prior to the January 3 date, Mayor Longshore
had arranged that if she were to take the position it would not
occur until the early part of February, hoping that in the mean-
time, we would be in receipt of your opinion.  Thus, for the
record, Mayor Longshore has not been employed by the Bay Area
Council, and as a result of your letter of January 19, 1983, will
not accept any position with the Bay Area Council.

     Sincerely yours,


     [signature]
KENNETH C. SCHEDIG
City Attorney

KCS/jr

cc:  The Honorable Diane Longshore, Mayor
     Kate Sproul, League of California Cities
     Natalie West, City Attorney, Berkeley


CONCORD CIVIC CENTER        1950 PARKSIDE DRIVE        CONCORD CALIFORNIA 94519

PHONE: (415) 671-                                                CITY COUNCIL

          3160                                            Diane Longshore, Mayor
                                                          Stephen L. Weir, Vice Mayor
Office of City Attorney                                   June V. Buiman
                                                          Colleen Cole
                                                          Ronald K. Mullin
                                                          Farrel A. Stewart, City Manager
December 23, 1982


Janis Shank McLean, Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Re:  Employment of Diane Longshore by Bay Area Council

Dear Ms. McLean:

     This office requests an opinion regarding the limitations which
the provisions of Government Code Section 87100-87103 would place
upon Mayor Diane Longshore if she is employed by a non-profit
corporation known as the Bay Area Council.

BACKGROUND.

     The City of Concord is a general law city in Contra Costa Coun-
ty.  Diane Longshore is a member of the City Council and has been
selected by her colleagues to act as Mayor until January 23,
1984.  The duties performed by Ms. Longshore as Mayor do not sub-
stantially affect the issues raised in this request.  As a Coun-
cilmember, Ms. Longshore deals with a panoply of issues and prob-
lems which confront the City.  Land use decision is one of the
most important areas over which the City Council exercises its
legislative authority.

     Recently, Mayor Longshore was offered a position with the Bay
Area Council to act as field liaison for that organization's
Housing Advocacy Program.  For your information, I am enclosing
pertinent portions of the 1980-81 BAC Annual Report which identi-
fies the organizational structure and purposes of BAC; the hous-
ing Section which delineates the goals of the organization; and
the job description of the position offered to Mayor Longshore.
You will note that Ms. Longshore will enter into a contract with
BAC.  This position was offered to the Mayor because her politi-
cal philosophy on housing is consistent with the position of the
BAC.


CONCORD CIVIC CENTER        1950 PARKSIDE DRIVE        CONCORD CALIFORNIA 94519

Me. Janie Shank McLean
December 23, 1982
Page 2


PROBLEM.

The problem confronted by Mayor Longshore is whether employment
with BAC would constitute a conflict under Section 87103(c), thus
prohibiting her from participating in any discussions and/or decisions of the City Council on issues dealing with or impacting
upon housing. For example, the City Council is presently receiving testimony on the establishment of an off-site street improvement fee which will be used to construct future roads needed because of new development. The staff has recommended the imposition of a substantial fee upon new development and this, according to testimony of builders, will adversely affect the housing
market. Would the Mayor be prohibited from participating in this
important issue because of her employment with BAC? Would the
Mayor be prohibited from participating in other decisions regarding the elements of the City's general plan, or subdivision approval, variances, use permits, or zoning if they might be consistent with or contrary to the philosophical position of the BAC?

The problem presented herein differs from the usual questions
poised to the Commission because it deals with the extent that
the philosophy of an organization can constitute a conflict for a
public official. As indicated above, the Mayor's philosophy regarding housing issues is similar to the principles of the BAC
and she is presently entitled to vote her convictions without
disqualification. Should her employment with a non-profit organization which espouses general principles regarding housing, or
which takes a position on agenda issues affecting housing, suddenly create a conflict?

If the Mayor were employed by a land developer whose interests
were directly affected by an issue before the City Council, it
would be clear that a conflict would exist. However, I question
the application of the nexus test of Regulation 18702 to Mayor
Longshore should she begin working for the BAC. While she will
be paid more than $250 by this organization, a decision she renders as a City Councilmember is not ipso facto tied to the purposes for which she receives this income. If the BAC took a specific position against the aforementioned offset street fee program, then a conflict might exist because her duties require her to "testify on positions taken by the Council." However, if the
BAC has a general position against the imposition of fees on new
development, I would not conclude that a conflict exists. Furthermore, since Mayor Longshore would be a contractual employee
whose employment is not contingent on her votes, the possibility
of voting a specific way in order to keep her job doesn't exist.

MS. Janie Shank McLean
December 23, 1982
Page 3


Finally, if it is your opinion that a conflict would exist should
the Mayor accept the field representative position with the PAC,
would your position be any different if the Mayor's activities
did not include work in Contra Costa County?

If there is any additional information which I can provide to you
regarding the above, please advise. Your prompt consideration of
this request would be appreciated since her job would commence on
January 3, 1983.

Sincerely yours,

[illegible signature]
KENNETH C. SCHEIDIG
City Attorney

KCS/jr
Enc.

cc: Mayor Diane Longshore

BAY
AREA
COUNCIL

POSITION DESCRIPTION: FIELD LIAISON
HOUSING ACTION PROGRAM, BAY AREA COUNCIL


General Duties:
Act as a field representative for the Bay Area Council's Housing Advocacy
Program.


Responsibilities:
1) Monitor priority Council housing policy deliberations at planning
commissions, city councils and boards of supervisors.

2) Testify on positions taken by the Council.

3) Coordinate and recommend positions for the Council.

4) Prepare and brief company representatives that are requested to
advocate on Council priority policies.

5) Assist in the development and expansion of local housing coalitions
that have been identified by the Council.

6) Create opportunities for Council involvement with local groups acting
on housing matters.


Organizational and Reporting:
1) The Field Liaison reports to the Housing Program Director and will
have substantial contact with the Policy Analyst.

2) All projects for activity will be pre-selected, but if new local
issues arise the Field Liaison is expected to recommend them.

3) The position is a 20-hour (approximately) part-time contractual position.
Expenses covered by the Council include: $.20 per mile, phone calls
and occasional lunch meetings. There are no set hours.

4) The fee will be $1,000.00 per month for 4 months. The contract will
be reviewed on April 1, 1983. [illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]


THE BAY AREA COUNCIL, INC.    345 CALIFORNIA CENTER    SAN FRANCISCO 94111    (415) 981-6405

of the Bay Area Council

To be funded largely by special corporate contributions and foundation grants, the Bay Area action program calls for the Council to:

• organize and assist local pro-housing coalitions, establishing an informally structured Regional Housing Network to serve as a rallying point, resource and clearinghouse for groups making grassroots efforts to increase housing supply in their own communities.

• encourage the business community to become involved. Corporations have a sizable stake in solving housing problems, as well as the human and technical resources to make a real contribution. The Council is already in the process of producing a Housing Action Guide for the business community that will inform corporations and business groups about the major ways they can help increase housing supply, genuine from common cations and technical assistance programs to political activism and innovative ways of actually getting more housing built. He action guide will be the centerp in the Council's efforts to enlist the business community's support and participation.

• convene public/private forums on growth issues. A series of subregional workshops will be held to increase communication among government officials, top corporate management, and industrial and residential developers, in order to identify both opportunities and obstacles to pal and housing growth.

• conduct a campaign to encourage balanced media coverage of housing issues, increased public awareness of housing needs, and more positive attitudes toward new housing, are essential. Toward this end, the Council will work with the media, and assist public- and media-relations activities of local pro-housing groups.

• selectively advocate local, regional, and state housing policies. In some cases, the Council will be directly involved in advocacy, force-line its efforts to achieve precedent-setting policy changes.

There can be no doubt that two years simply isn't ample time to solve a problem that has been years in the making, nor can how broad-based or effective the campaign. The Council will must ev- antly be involved in housing issues well into the '80s. Its Housing Act ' Program is not an end, but a beginning.

5

1980-81 Report

Housing

Housing continues to top the Council's agenda, and the BAC's multi-phased housing project has reached a new turning point. A year has been spent studying regional housing problems and their possible solutions. Now concern is being translated into action, as the Council begins a major effort to increase the supply of affordable housing in the Bay Area.

Last year, the Council undertook a study of regional housing problems based on the belief that the corporate community had an obligation - both to itself and to the region as a whole - to become involved. It was already becoming apparent that the high cost and inadequate supply of housing are a threat to the Bay Area's economic and social well-being. Business firms had begun reporting difficulties recruiting or transferring employees discouraged by the area's tight housing market, and the phrase "jobs/housing imbalance" was cropping up more and more frequently in discussions of corporate expansion plans.

After months of intensive study by the Council's broad-based 30-member Housing Task Force, the Council released findings that fully justify labelling the situation a Bay Area housing crisis.

A Call to Action

Due to an escalating shortage of housing, the Bay Area's home prices are the highest in the nation, with the median price 76% higher than that nationwide, and no end to the upward spiral in sight. Over the past five years, median home values have been increasing twice as fast as median household income; for renters the situation is nearly as bad. And the supply-demand gap continues to grow. The region is building about 10,000 fewer housing units per year than needed to meet demand. Most startling of all is how serious the problem could become in the not-too-distant future: if current trends are not reversed, the region will be some 350,000 housing units short by 1990.

Summarized in the Council's December, 1980 report, Housing: The Bay Area's Challenge of the '80s, the study supported the grim conclusion that "a worsening housing shortage could be disastrous, not only for those who are excluded from the housing market, but for whole communities, for businesses - ultimately, in one way or the other, for nearly everyone in the Bay Area."

Given extensive distribution and media coverage, the report helped raise awareness that it will take more than lower interest rates to head off the region's housing crisis. Action must be taken to change the local conditions - particularly restrictive public policies and anti-housing attitudes - that make the Bay Area's housing market the tightest in the country.

The Goal: a Catalyst for Change

The Bay Area Council is helping to meet this challenge with an ambitious two-year effort to increase housing densities, bring more land into residential development, change public attitudes, and help streamline and balance the local land-use decision-making process. Developed with input from some 50 of California's foremost experts in the housing field, the Council Housing Action Program aims to achieve change not just at the regional and state levels, but also in each of the nine counties and 96 cities of the Bay Area.

The Council will concentrate on providing the impetus, the organizational support, and the ammunition for action by an entire spectrum of current and potential constituents for new housing. An important element in the program will be ongoing cooperation with other groups also active in local, regional, and state housing issues. For example, the Council is working with a group of statewide organizations to create Californians for Housing, an independent, nonprofit housing research, advocacy, and education organization.

Building on
35 Years of Achievement

The Beginning
The Bay Area Council is created by a forward thinking group of business leaders to pioneer a regional approach to areawide problems, and ease the Bay Area's transition to a peacetime economy.

Creating the Framework
The Council contributes to creation of the Bay Area Air Pollution Control District, Citizens for Rapid Transit (the driving force behind BART), and other early efforts to address issues affecting the region's economy and environment.

Expanding the Regional Role
In the forefront of areawide planning efforts, the Council is instrumental in passage of the BART bond issue, creation of the Bay Area Transportation Study Commission, the first comprehensive look at regional transportation issues, and passage of legislation creating the Bay Conservation and Development Commission. The BAC also becomes the Bay Area's leading exponent of a comprehensive regional agency to coordinate planning and decision making.

Working for Balance
With an ever widening scope of involvement in regional issues, the Council focuses on the need for policies and institutional mechanisms balancing economic, environmental and social goals. In the course of the decade, the BAC works for creation of the Metropolitan Transportation Commission; participates in development of regional plans for transportation, land use, water-related development, air quality, and water quality; coordinates business input to legislation on coastal regulation and environmental impact; sponsors influential studies on transit finance, maritime development, energy conservation, solid waste, and the regional economy. Given the increasing effects of federal, state, and local government regulation on the Bay Area economy, the Council's special mission is to make economic growth a positive goal reflected in public policy.

Meeting New Challenges
In an era of increasingly complex issues and choices, the Council is not only following up on past programs, but initiating involvements in new areas, notably housing. A bridge between the public and private sector, a catalyst for regional cooperation, the Council is helping to make the tough decisions that will shape the Bay Area of the '80s, the '90s, and beyond.

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

January 19, 1983


Mr. Kenneth C. Scheidig
City Attorney
Concord Civic Center
1950 Parkside Drive
Concord, CA  94519

Re:  Your Request for Advice, Our
     Advice No. A-82-212

Dear Mr. Scheidig:

     I am writing in response to your request for advice on
behalf of the City of Concord's mayor, Diane Longshore.  The
facts, as presented to me, are that Mayor Longshore is employed
by the Bay Area Council (hereinafter BAC), a non-profit corporation, as a field liaison for its Housing Advocacy Program.  The
goals of the Program are to "increase housing densities; bring
more land into residential development; change public attitudes;
and help streamline and balance the local land-use decision-
making process."1/  The Program promotes change at the
regional and state level, as well as in the nine counties and 96
cities of the Bay Area.2/

     Mayor Longshore's position as a field liaison involves, among
other things, monitoring priority BAC housing policy deliberations at planning commission, city council and board of
supervisors meetings; testifying on positions taken by BAC;
working with others who are promoting the positions of BAC; and
coordinating and recommending positions for BAC.3/

___________________

1/  1980-1982 Report of the Bay Area Council.

2/  1980-1981 Report.

3/  BAC becomes directly involved in advocacy, targeting
its efforts to achieve precedent-setting policy changes.
(1980-1982 Report.)

Mr. Kenneth C. Scheidig
January 19, 1983
Page 2


Government Code Section 87100⁴/ provides that:

     No public official ... shall make, participate in
the making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

An official has a financial interest within the meaning of
Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision
will have a material financial effect on;

     (c)  Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or
more ... received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.

                              Section 87103.

The Bay Area Council has either promised to provide, or has
provided, Mayor Longshore with $250 or more in income.⁵/
Thus, it is a "source of income" to her.

The Commission, by regulation, 2 California Administrative
Code Section 18702, has defined several ways in which a decision
can have a material financial effect on a source of income.
Under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(B), a decision has a
material financial effect on an official's source of income when
"there is a nexus between a governmental decision and the purpose for which the official receives income...." This regulation, in effect, prevents Mayor Longshore from accomplishing as
an official what she is paid to do in her private position.⁵/


_________________

4/  Hereinafter all statutory references made are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

5/  She recently began working for BAC and you did not
indicate now much she has received under the contract.  However,
it is undisputed that she will received $250 or more in income.

6/  Of course, Mayor Longshore is also required to abstain
from a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on BAC.  (See
Sections 87100, 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.)

Mr. Kenneth C. Scheidig
January 19, 1983
Page 3


Mayor Longshore's position as field liaison for BAC's Housing
Advocacy Program includes developing and promoting the policies
and positions of BAC relating to housing. Thus, as a general
ruler, under the "nexus test", she may not make or participate in
the making of governmental decisions, including decisions on the
general plan, subdivision approvals, variances, use permits, or
zoning, which may further these policies or positions.1/  More
specifically, Mayor Longshore must abstain from:

1.  Housing decisions before the Concord City Council on
which BAC has taken a particular position; or

2.  Housing decisions concerning matters on which BAC has a
specific policy or position; or

3.  Housing decisions concerning matters on which BAC has a
general policy that clearly implies a specific result on a
decision.

In your letter, you asked how the Act's prohibitions would
apply to a proposed off-street improvement fee which is coming
before the Concord City Council.2/  Mayor Longshore must
abstain from this decision if BAC has taken a position on
Concord's proposal; BAC has taken a specific position on
off-street improvement fees; or, if BAC has a general policy
which dictates a certain outcome, regardless of other con-
siderations (such as a policy of being against any fees on
housing developments).

_______________________

1/  "There is an inherent appearance of conflict as well
as an actual conflict when an official is being paid to promote
or represent certain positions, on the one hand, and on the
other, is being called on to determine public policies and
positions in the same area".  (See the enclosed copy of the
Commission's advice letter to Linda Best, Aug. 21, 1981, p.2.)

Participation in a governmental decision includes not only
voting, but also participating in deliberations and conferring
with other members of the governing body.  (See 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18700 (copy enclosed).)

2/  The off-site street improvement fee would be used to
construct roads necessary for new developments.  Builders argue
that the fee will adversely affect the housing market.

Mr. Kenneth C. Schneidig
January 19, 1983
Page 4


     City Council decisions on elements of the general plan,
subdivision approvals, variances, use permits, or zoning also
require Mayor Longshore's disqualification, if BAC has taken a
position on a decision before the Council, if BAC has a specific
policy regarding the subject matter of that decision, or a
general policy which requires a certain outcome.

     You asked whether the application of the Political Reform
Act would be affected if Mayor Longshore confined her activities
for BAC to areas outside of Contra Costa County. Our analysis
would be unchanged, regardless of any limitation on the area in
which she works for BAC, so long as she is developing and
promoting policies and proposals that have Bay Area-wide
application. However, to the extent that decisions in Concord
are unrelated to BAC's Bay Area-wide policies, our analysis with
respect to a specific decision might be different. If such a
situation arises, I would recommend that you seek our advice.

     I hope I have clarified this matter. If I can be of any
additional help, please feel free to contact me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:km

embedding.qa_text: This is a letter from the City of Concord, dated January 25, 1983, addressed to Janis Shank McLean, Counsel for the Legal Division of the State of California Fair Political Practices Commission. The letter responds to a request for advice regarding the employment of Diane Longshore by the Bay Area Council. The letter states that in regard to Janis Shank McLean's letter of January 19, 1983, it was indicated in the first paragraph that Mayor Longshore is employed by the Bay Area Council. However, due to the fact that it would not be possible to get the advice letter prior to the January 3 date, Mayor Longshore had arranged that if she were to take the position, it would not occur until the early part of February, hoping that in the meantime, they would be in receipt of your opinion. Thus, for the record, Mayor Longshore has not been employed by the Bay Area Council, and as a result of your letter of January 19, 1983, will not accept any position with the Bay Area Council. Sincerely yours, KENNETH C.
